subject: general-knowledge
description: United Nations & Organizations MCQs for PPSC, FPSC, NTS, CSS & PMS.

mcqs.q: When was 'The United Nations Declaration' signed?
mcqs.explanation: The UN Declaration was signed on 1 January 1942 by 26 nations during WWII, pledging to fight the Axis powers.

mcqs.q: When was the United Nations officially set up?
mcqs.explanation: The UN was established on 24 October 1945 after ratification of its Charter — celebrated annually as UN Day.

mcqs.q: Who signed the 'United Nations Declaration'?
options: President Roosevelt and Churchill PM of Britain
mcqs.explanation: The UN Declaration of 1942 was signed by Roosevelt (USA), Churchill (UK), Litvinov (USSR), T. V. Soong (China) and others.

mcqs.q: How many states signed the 'United Nations Declaration' on 1 January 1942?
mcqs.explanation: 26 nations signed the UN Declaration on 1 January 1942 (note: the original CSS-style answer key gives 24; modern sources say 26).

mcqs.q: Who presided at the San Francisco Conference which became the main thrust to build UNO?
mcqs.explanation: Lord Halifax presided at the San Francisco Conference (1945) where the UN Charter was drafted.

mcqs.q: The World Economic Outlook Report is issued annually by:
mcqs.explanation: The World Economic Outlook is the IMF's flagship publication, issued annually (with updates).

mcqs.q: Currently how many states have observer status in the UN?
mcqs.explanation: Two non-member observer states: the Holy See (Vatican City) and the State of Palestine.

mcqs.q: Which reason is indicated for the birth of the UN in the Preamble of the UN Charter?
mcqs.explanation: The Preamble cites the experience of a devastating war (WWII) as the principal motivation for the UN.

mcqs.q: The term 'United Nations' was coined by:
mcqs.explanation: US President Franklin D. Roosevelt coined the term 'United Nations' in 1942.

mcqs.q: With the addition of South Sudan on 13 July 2011, the United Nations now has how many member states?
mcqs.explanation: South Sudan brought UN membership to 193, the current total.

mcqs.q: United Nations headquarters is situated in:
mcqs.explanation: The UN HQ is in New York, USA. Other UN offices are at Geneva, Vienna and Nairobi.

mcqs.q: The USA is the largest contributor to the UN regular budget. Which is the second largest contributor?
mcqs.explanation: Historically Japan was the second-largest contributor (now overtaken by China in some periods).

mcqs.q: How many representatives to the General Assembly does each participating country have?
mcqs.explanation: Each UN member state may send up to 5 representatives to the General Assembly but has only 1 vote.

mcqs.q: Which UN agency looks after the welfare of workers?
mcqs.explanation: The International Labour Organization (ILO), founded in 1919, looks after workers' welfare.

mcqs.q: Who was the first Secretary-General of the United Nations?
mcqs.explanation: Trygve Lie of Norway was the first UN Secretary-General (1946-1952).

mcqs.q: Name the only Secretary-General of the United Nations who resigned from his post:
mcqs.explanation: Trygve Lie is the only UN Secretary-General to have resigned (in 1952).

mcqs.q: Ban Ki-moon was the second Asian Secretary-General. Who was the first Asian Secretary-General?
mcqs.explanation: U Thant of Myanmar (then Burma) was the first Asian UN Secretary-General (1961-1971).

mcqs.q: Official languages of the UN are:
options: Chinese, English, French, Russian and Spanish
English, French, Spanish, Russian and Urdu
mcqs.explanation: The 6 UN official languages are Arabic, Chinese, English, French, Russian and Spanish.

mcqs.q: Arabic was added to UN official languages in which year?
mcqs.explanation: Arabic was added to the UN's official languages in 1973.

mcqs.q: How many official languages are there in the UN?
mcqs.explanation: There are 6 official languages: Arabic, Chinese, English, French, Russian, Spanish.

mcqs.q: Which two languages are used by the UN Secretariat as working languages?
mcqs.explanation: The UN Secretariat uses English and French as its two working languages.

mcqs.q: Who addressed the UN in English, French, Spanish, Russian, Arabic and Chinese in 1995?
mcqs.explanation: Pope John Paul II addressed the UN in all 6 official languages in October 1995.

mcqs.q: On 15 March 2006 the UN General Assembly voted to replace the UN Commission on Human Rights with:
mcqs.explanation: The UN Human Rights Council (HRC) replaced the older Commission on Human Rights in 2006.

mcqs.q: Members of the UN Human Rights Council are elected for a term of:
mcqs.explanation: HRC members are elected by the General Assembly for 3-year terms.

mcqs.q: The presidency of the UN Security Council rotates alphabetically every:
mcqs.explanation: The SC presidency rotates monthly in English alphabetical order of members.

mcqs.q: The first UN peacekeeping mission (1948) is concerned with which conflict?
mcqs.explanation: UNTSO (UN Truce Supervision Organization), 1948, was the first UN peacekeeping mission — for the Israeli-Palestinian conflict.

mcqs.q: Besides UNTSO, which two other UN peacekeeping missions are currently ongoing in the Middle East?
mcqs.explanation: UNIFIL (Lebanon) and UNDOF (Golan Heights, Syria) are the other ongoing Middle-East UN missions.

mcqs.q: As of 2009, which continent hosted the majority of ongoing UN peacekeeping missions?
mcqs.explanation: Africa hosted most ongoing UN peacekeeping missions as of 2009.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is NOT a permanent member of the Security Council?
mcqs.explanation: The P5 are China, France, Russia, UK and USA. Germany is not a permanent member.

mcqs.q: Which US President gave the United Nations its name?
mcqs.explanation: Franklin D. Roosevelt coined the name 'United Nations' in 1942.

mcqs.q: Where is the United Nations Building located?
mcqs.explanation: The UN headquarters building is in New York City, USA.

mcqs.q: Which UN organization is called the 'World Bank'?
mcqs.explanation: The International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD) is commonly known as the World Bank.

mcqs.q: How many countries were the original members of the United Nations when it was first established?
mcqs.explanation: The UN had 51 original members in 1945.

mcqs.q: Which institution of the UN is described as a 'parliament of nations' meeting to consider pressing problems?
mcqs.explanation: The General Assembly, where all 193 members are represented, is often called the parliament of nations.

mcqs.q: Which institution carries out the day-to-day running of the UN?
mcqs.explanation: The UN Secretariat (led by the Secretary-General) carries out day-to-day administration.

mcqs.q: The Security Council has 15 members, 5 of whom are permanent. Which of the following is NOT a permanent member?
mcqs.explanation: The P5 are China, France, UK, USA and Russia. Germany is NOT a permanent member.

mcqs.q: UNESCO stands for:
options: UN Educational Scientific and Cultural Organization
mcqs.explanation: UNESCO = United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization, HQ Paris.

mcqs.q: Which UN agency protects children's rights and improves the health and education of young people?
mcqs.explanation: UNICEF (UN Children's Fund), founded 1946, looks after children's rights and welfare.

mcqs.q: In countries where there has been a war, UN soldiers sent to prevent violence are called:
mcqs.explanation: UN soldiers deployed to monitor ceasefires are called UN Peacekeeping Troops.

mcqs.q: Who became the Secretary-General of the UN on 1 January 1997?
mcqs.explanation: Kofi Annan of Ghana became UN Secretary-General on 1 January 1997 (served 1997-2006).

mcqs.q: What is the name of the document setting out the main aims of the UN?
mcqs.explanation: The founding document of the UN is the United Nations Charter (signed 26 June 1945).

mcqs.q: Which is the recently created UN institution as part of the Human Rights machinery?
mcqs.explanation: The UN Human Rights Council (HRC) was created in 2006 as the most recent UN human-rights body.

mcqs.q: The Security Council consists of 15 seats. Which country does NOT have a permanent seat?
mcqs.explanation: India does not have a permanent seat. The P5 are China, France, Russia, UK and USA.

mcqs.q: Which UN organization provides facilities for arbitration of disputes between governments and foreign investors?
mcqs.explanation: ICSID — International Centre for Settlement of Investment Disputes — handles investment arbitration. Part of the World Bank Group.

mcqs.q: Which of these countries was NOT an original member of the UN?
mcqs.explanation: Italy was not among the 51 original UN members in 1945 (it joined in 1955).

mcqs.q: Which of these countries was an original member in 1945?
mcqs.explanation: Iraq was one of the 51 original UN members in 1945.

mcqs.q: In which city was the United Nations created?
mcqs.explanation: The UN Charter was drafted and signed at the San Francisco Conference (April-June 1945).

mcqs.q: Who inspired the creation of the United Nations?
mcqs.explanation: Franklin D. Roosevelt was the chief inspirer of the UN — he coined its name in 1942.

mcqs.q: What does the UN Charter call for?
options: Save succeeding generations from the scourge of war
mcqs.explanation: The Preamble pledges 'to save succeeding generations from the scourge of war'.

mcqs.q: Which five nations argued they deserve greater say in the Security Council?
options: Brazil, Germany, Japan, S. Africa, Nigeria
mcqs.explanation: The G4 (Brazil, Germany, India, Japan) + Nigeria have pushed for permanent UNSC seats.

mcqs.q: In which two years has the UN sanctioned full-scale wars?
mcqs.explanation: Korean War 1950 and Gulf War 1991 — the two UN-sanctioned full-scale wars.

mcqs.q: In which four world areas was the UN undertaking peacekeeping operations in 2009?
options: Africa, Asia, Europe and the Middle East
Europe, N. America, S. America and Middle East
Africa, Asia, N. America and Middle East
Asia, Africa, S. America and Middle East
mcqs.explanation: In 2009 UN peacekeeping operations were in Africa, Asia, Europe and the Middle East.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is NOT one of the main 'organs' of the United Nations?
mcqs.explanation: There are 6 principal organs of the UN. 'Humanitarian Affairs Council' is not one of them.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is a specialized agency of the United Nations?
mcqs.explanation: WHO is a specialized agency of the UN; the Red Cross is an independent humanitarian movement.

mcqs.q: On what date is United Nations Day observed?
mcqs.explanation: UN Day is observed on 24 October, the date the UN Charter entered into force in 1945.

mcqs.q: Where is the head office of the UN High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR)?
mcqs.explanation: UNHCR is headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland (founded 1950).

mcqs.q: UNAIDS stands for:
mcqs.explanation: UNAIDS is the Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS.

mcqs.q: Where does the International Court of Justice sit?
mcqs.explanation: The ICJ — principal judicial organ of the UN — sits at the Peace Palace in The Hague.

mcqs.q: How many representatives does each country have to the International Labour Organization (ILO)?
mcqs.explanation: Each ILO member country sends 4 representatives — 2 government, 1 employer, 1 worker (tripartite structure).

mcqs.q: Which 'technical organization' coordinates postal policies with its members?
mcqs.explanation: The Universal Postal Union (UPU, est. 1874) coordinates international postal services. HQ: Bern, Switzerland.

mcqs.q: Which UN specialized organization facilitates sharing of weather and climate information?
mcqs.explanation: The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) facilitates global weather and climate information sharing. HQ: Geneva.

mcqs.q: Where is the UN International Crime and Justice Research Institute located?
mcqs.explanation: UNICRI is located in Turin, Italy.

mcqs.q: Which are the autonomous institutions for training and research within the UN?
mcqs.explanation: UNITAR, UNIDIR, UNRISD, UN-INSTRAW, UN University and UPEACE are all UN training/research institutions.

mcqs.q: What international organization promotes peaceful use of nuclear energy?
mcqs.explanation: The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) promotes peaceful use of nuclear energy. HQ: Vienna.

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of the UN Environmental Program (UNEP)?
mcqs.explanation: UNEP is headquartered in Nairobi, Kenya — the only major UN agency HQ in Africa.

mcqs.q: Where is the World Health Organization based?
mcqs.explanation: WHO is headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland (founded 7 April 1948).

mcqs.q: What is the full form of IAEA?
mcqs.explanation: IAEA = International Atomic Energy Agency, HQ Vienna.

mcqs.q: IFAD stands for:
mcqs.explanation: IFAD = International Fund for Agricultural Development, HQ Rome (founded 1977).

mcqs.q: ICAO stands for:
options: International Committee for Approval of Organizations
mcqs.explanation: ICAO = International Civil Aviation Organization, HQ Montreal, Canada (1944).

mcqs.q: What is the foremost important purpose of the UN?
mcqs.explanation: The UN's foremost purpose, per Article 1 of the Charter, is to maintain international peace and security.

mcqs.q: When the San Francisco Conference adopted the UN Charter, how many nations signed it?
mcqs.explanation: 50 nations signed the UN Charter at the San Francisco Conference on 26 June 1945. Poland signed later, making 51 originals.

mcqs.q: Amendment to Article 23 of the UN Charter enlarged the membership of the:
mcqs.explanation: The 1963 amendment to Article 23 enlarged the Security Council from 11 to 15 members.

mcqs.q: Where is the International Criminal Court (ICC) located?
mcqs.explanation: The ICC is located in The Hague, Netherlands. (Note: the ICC is separate from the UN.)

mcqs.q: Which Act was created by the Indian Parliament on the subject of the UN?
mcqs.explanation: The United Nations (Privileges & Immunities) Act, 1947 implements the UN's immunities in India.

mcqs.q: In which article of the UN Charter are the purposes of the UN enshrined?
mcqs.explanation: Article 1 of the UN Charter sets out the purposes of the UN.

mcqs.q: Which part of the UN Charter contains the phrase 'to save the succeeding generations from the scourge of war'?
mcqs.explanation: This famous phrase appears in the Preamble of the UN Charter.

mcqs.q: What is the second purpose of the UN Charter?
options: Develop friendly relations among world leaders
Develop friendly relations among nations based on equal rights and self determination
mcqs.explanation: Per Article 1(2), the second purpose is to develop friendly relations among nations based on equal rights and self-determination.

mcqs.q: Which Article of the UN Charter deals with international economic and social co-operation?
mcqs.explanation: Article 55 sets out the UN's commitments to international economic and social co-operation.

mcqs.q: What are the principal organs of the UN?
mcqs.explanation: The six principal organs of the UN are GA, SC, ECOSOC, Trusteeship Council, ICJ and the Secretariat.

mcqs.q: What is the status of the UN?
mcqs.explanation: The UN is a democratic international organization based on sovereign equality of its members.

mcqs.q: On what basis are important matters of the UN decided?
mcqs.explanation: Important matters are decided by majority vote (two-thirds majority in the General Assembly for major issues).

mcqs.q: Which organ of the UN decides all important matters of peace and security?
mcqs.explanation: The Security Council has primary responsibility for peace and security and decides those matters.

mcqs.q: When the General Assembly decides important matters, what majority is required?
mcqs.explanation: A two-thirds majority of members present and voting is required for important General Assembly decisions.

mcqs.q: What is the primary responsibility of the Security Council?
options: Maintain peace and security in the world
mcqs.explanation: Per Article 24, the SC has the primary responsibility for the maintenance of international peace and security.

mcqs.q: In the UN, who is empowered to use armed forces for maintenance of peace and security?
mcqs.explanation: Under Chapter VII of the Charter, the Security Council may authorize use of armed force.

mcqs.q: Who is bound to provide armed forces to the UN?
mcqs.explanation: Per Article 43, all member states and especially SC members are obliged to make armed forces available to the UN.

mcqs.q: Which is the principal judicial organ of the UN?
mcqs.explanation: The International Court of Justice (ICJ) is the principal judicial organ of the UN. HQ: The Hague.

mcqs.q: When was the International Criminal Court (ICC) established by treaty?
mcqs.explanation: The Rome Statute establishing the ICC was negotiated between 15 June and 17 July 1998 (commonly cited 18 June - 19 August 1998).

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of the World Meteorological Organization?
mcqs.explanation: WMO HQ is in Geneva, Switzerland.

mcqs.q: According to the UN Charter, how many types of members are there?
mcqs.explanation: Two types: Original members and States admitted afterwards under Article 4.

mcqs.q: What are the types of membership category defined by the Charter of the UN?
mcqs.explanation: The Charter recognises original members (1945) and members admitted under Article 4.

mcqs.q: Which UN body decides if a state is peace-loving and willing to carry out UN Charter obligations?
mcqs.explanation: Admission requires recommendation by the SC and approval by the GA.

mcqs.q: Which article of the UN Charter mentions expulsion of a member from the UN?
mcqs.explanation: Article 6 allows the GA to expel a member that has persistently violated the principles of the Charter, on the SC's recommendation.

mcqs.q: On whose recommendation does the UN General Assembly decide suspension of a state from membership?
mcqs.explanation: Suspension under Article 5 occurs on the recommendation of the Security Council.

mcqs.q: What majority is required when the General Assembly decides suspension of a state from UN membership?
mcqs.explanation: A two-thirds majority of members present and voting is required.

mcqs.q: In the UN General Assembly, how many representatives can come from a member state?
mcqs.explanation: Each member can send up to 5 representatives but has only 1 vote.

mcqs.q: How many votes can be exercised by a member state in the General Assembly?
mcqs.explanation: Each member state has 1 vote in the General Assembly — irrespective of size, wealth or contribution.

mcqs.q: What majority is required for member states when taking decisions on important issues?
mcqs.explanation: Important questions in the General Assembly require a two-thirds majority.

mcqs.q: Which UN body draws the Security Council's attention to situations endangering international peace?
mcqs.explanation: The General Assembly may draw the Security Council's attention to international situations under Article 11.

mcqs.q: Which Article provides that the GA shall initiate studies promoting international co-operation?
mcqs.explanation: Article 13 empowers the GA to initiate studies and recommendations for international co-operation.

mcqs.q: Which of the following has been awarded the Nobel Peace Prize?
mcqs.explanation: The ILO was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1969 on its 50th anniversary.

mcqs.q: On whose internal affairs shall the UN General Assembly not pass any resolution?
mcqs.explanation: Article 2(7) of the Charter prohibits the UN from intervening in matters essentially within a state's domestic jurisdiction.

mcqs.q: Which organs are subordinate to the UN?
mcqs.explanation: Both ECOSOC and the Trusteeship Council are subsidiary organs of the UN system.

mcqs.q: Which organ of the UN approves the UN budget?
mcqs.explanation: The General Assembly approves the UN regular budget under Article 17.

mcqs.q: Which Article provides that the General Assembly shall consider and approve the budget borne by the members?
mcqs.explanation: Article 17 vests budgetary authority in the General Assembly.

mcqs.q: In which year did the UN pass a resolution establishing the International Law Commission?
mcqs.explanation: The General Assembly created the International Law Commission in 1948 to codify international law.

mcqs.q: In which year was the UN Institute for Training and Research (UNITAR) established?
mcqs.explanation: UNITAR was established in 1965 by the General Assembly.

mcqs.q: Which UN institution decided that UN administrative tribunal rulings shall be binding on member states?
mcqs.explanation: An ICJ advisory opinion held UN Administrative Tribunal rulings binding.

mcqs.q: In which year did the UN establish the UN Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD)?
mcqs.explanation: UNCTAD was established in 1964 as a permanent intergovernmental body.

mcqs.q: When did the UN General Assembly pass the 'Uniting for Peace Resolution'?
mcqs.explanation: The Uniting for Peace Resolution (377(V)) was passed on 3 November 1950 (commonly cited 4 Nov).

mcqs.q: When can a special emergency session of the General Assembly be called?
options: Majority of members of the General Assembly
mcqs.explanation: An emergency session can be requested by 9 SC members or by a majority of GA members.

mcqs.q: Which UN body may consider the matter if the Security Council fails to prevent aggression or breach of peace?
mcqs.explanation: Per the Uniting for Peace resolution, the GA may consider it if the SC is unable to act due to veto.

mcqs.q: How many members does the UN Collective Measures Committee have for examining peace and security matters?
mcqs.explanation: The Collective Measures Committee was established with 15 members.

mcqs.q: Which UN member country challenged and criticized the 'UN Resolution Uniting for Peace'?
mcqs.explanation: The USSR strongly criticised the Uniting for Peace resolution as bypassing its Security Council veto.

mcqs.q: Who appoints the Secretary-General of the UN?
options: UN General Assembly upon recommendation of Security Council
mcqs.explanation: The SG is appointed by the GA upon recommendation of the SC (subject to P5 veto).

mcqs.q: Under which Article of the UN Charter is the Secretary-General appointed?
mcqs.explanation: Article 97 governs the appointment of the Secretary-General.

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of the International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD)?
mcqs.explanation: IFAD HQ is in Rome, Italy (alongside FAO).

mcqs.q: What majority is required when the General Assembly deprives a member state for non-payment of financial contribution?
mcqs.explanation: A two-thirds majority is required to deprive a member of its vote under Article 19.

mcqs.q: Who elects ten non-permanent members of the Security Council?
mcqs.explanation: The 10 non-permanent SC members are elected by the General Assembly for 2-year terms.

mcqs.q: Under which Article of the UN Charter are the expenses of the Organization borne by members?
mcqs.explanation: Article 17(2) states expenses are borne by the members as apportioned by the General Assembly.

mcqs.q: Who elects the 54 members of the UN Economic and Social Council (ECOSOC)?
mcqs.explanation: ECOSOC's 54 members are elected by the General Assembly for 3-year terms.

mcqs.q: When was the UN International Drug Control Programme established?
mcqs.explanation: UNDCP was established in 1991/1993 (commonly cited 1993).

mcqs.q: In which Conference was it finally decided to establish a Security Council?
mcqs.explanation: The Security Council was established at the San Francisco Conference (April-June 1945).

mcqs.q: How many members are there in the Security Council?
mcqs.explanation: The Security Council has 15 members — 5 permanent + 10 non-permanent.

mcqs.q: How many permanent members exist in the Security Council?
mcqs.explanation: There are 5 permanent members of the SC (the P5).

mcqs.q: How many members are non-permanent in the Security Council?
mcqs.explanation: 10 non-permanent members are elected by the GA for 2-year terms.

mcqs.q: Who are the permanent members of the Security Council?
mcqs.explanation: The P5: China, Britain (UK), America (USA), Russia and France.

mcqs.q: On what date did the Security Council come into existence?
mcqs.explanation: The Security Council came into existence on 12 January 1946 — alongside the first General Assembly meeting.

mcqs.q: On 12 January 1946, the UN General Assembly elected how many non-permanent members of the Security Council?
mcqs.explanation: Originally 6 non-permanent members were elected. The number was raised to 10 in 1965.

mcqs.q: On what date did the General Assembly amend Article 23 to extend the Security Council to 15 members?
mcqs.explanation: The amendment was adopted on 17 December 1963 (came into force 31 August 1965).

mcqs.q: For what period are the ten non-permanent members elected by the General Assembly?
mcqs.explanation: Non-permanent SC members are elected for 2-year terms.

mcqs.q: Which type of members in the Security Council have veto power?
mcqs.explanation: Only the 5 permanent members (P5) have veto power.

mcqs.q: When is 'veto not considered as action of a permanent member'?
options: Absence of an SC permanent member during the SC meeting
mcqs.explanation: If a P5 member is absent or abstains, it is not treated as a veto (the 'Yalta formula').

mcqs.q: What are the functions and powers of the Security Council?
options: Maintenance of international peace, elective functions
mcqs.explanation: The SC has maintenance, elective, supervisory and constituent functions.

mcqs.q: When was the UN Children's Fund (UNICEF) established?
mcqs.explanation: UNICEF was established on 11 December 1946 (sometimes cited 1947).

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO)?
mcqs.explanation: ICAO is headquartered in Montreal, Canada.

mcqs.q: What is the function of the UN Population Fund (UNFPA) for developing nations?
options: Increasing quality of reproductive health service
Ending gender discrimination, formulating population policies
mcqs.explanation: UNFPA covers reproductive health, gender equality and population policy.

mcqs.q: When was the UN Environment Programme (UNEP) established?
mcqs.explanation: UNEP was established in 1972 after the Stockholm Conference. HQ: Nairobi.

mcqs.q: The Charter of the United Nations consists of:
mcqs.explanation: The UN Charter has 111 articles plus a Preamble.

mcqs.q: Where is the UN International Drug Control Programme headquartered?
mcqs.explanation: The UN International Drug Control Programme (now part of UNODC) is based in Vienna, Austria.

mcqs.q: Which members in the Security Council are conferred the veto power?
mcqs.explanation: Only the P5 — China, UK, USA, Russia and France — have veto power.

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (World Bank)?
mcqs.explanation: The World Bank (IBRD) is headquartered in Washington D.C., USA.

mcqs.q: Which UN organ separately elects the judges of the International Court of Justice?
mcqs.explanation: ICJ judges are elected by both the GA and the SC voting independently.

mcqs.q: To which UN organ does the Security Council send its annual report?
mcqs.explanation: The SC submits annual reports to the General Assembly.

mcqs.q: How many permanent members must give affirmative votes on important Security Council decisions?
mcqs.explanation: Substantive SC decisions require 9 affirmative votes including ALL 5 permanent members (no veto).

mcqs.q: The United Nations Secretariat consists of:
mcqs.explanation: The Secretariat is organized into Departments and Offices headed by the Secretary-General.

mcqs.q: Which permanent member used double veto on the question of Greece, Czechoslovakia and Spain?
mcqs.explanation: The USSR used 'double veto' on the questions of Greece, Czechoslovakia and Spain in the early years.

mcqs.q: Who can challenge the decision of the President of the Security Council?
mcqs.explanation: Permanent members of the SC may challenge a presidential decision.

mcqs.q: In which subject is the Security Council's recommendation necessary for a General Assembly decision?
options: Suspension and expulsion of UN members
mcqs.explanation: SC recommendation is needed for new-member admission, suspension, expulsion and SG appointment.

mcqs.q: What is the present strength of members in the Economic and Social Council (ECOSOC)?
mcqs.explanation: ECOSOC has 54 members, elected by the General Assembly for 3-year terms.

mcqs.q: Before 31 August 1965, how many members were in the Economic and Social Council?
mcqs.explanation: ECOSOC originally had 18 members until expanded to 27 in 1965 and to 54 in 1973.

mcqs.q: How many votes are entitled for members of the UN Economic and Social Council?
mcqs.explanation: Each ECOSOC member has 1 vote.

mcqs.q: Which UN organization may initiate studies and forward reports on economic/social matters to the General Assembly?
mcqs.explanation: ECOSOC undertakes studies and reports on economic, social and related matters.

mcqs.q: How many Regional Commissions are established by the UN Economic and Social Council?
mcqs.explanation: ECOSOC has 5 Regional Commissions: ECA, ECE, ECLAC, ESCAP and ESCWA.

mcqs.q: Which are the regional Commissions of the UN Economic and Social Council?
mcqs.explanation: All four — ECE, ESCAP, ECA, ECLAC (plus ESCWA) — are ECOSOC Regional Commissions.

mcqs.q: In which year did the UN establish UNCTAD?
mcqs.explanation: UNCTAD was established in 1964.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is a UN Programme and NOT a specialized agency?
mcqs.explanation: UNEP is a UN Programme. UNESCO, WHO and ILO are specialized agencies.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is a UN specialized agency and NOT a programme?
mcqs.explanation: IBRD (World Bank) is a UN specialized agency. UNDP, UNIFEM and UNFPA are UN programmes/funds.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is part of the World Bank Group of five institutions?
mcqs.explanation: The World Bank Group includes IBRD, IDA, IFC, MIGA and ICSID. IDA is part of it.

mcqs.q: Which of the following was established at the Bretton Woods Conference in 1944?
mcqs.explanation: The IMF (and IBRD/World Bank) were established at the 1944 Bretton Woods Conference.

mcqs.q: How many votes does each member of the Trusteeship Council possess?
mcqs.explanation: Each Trusteeship Council member has 1 vote.

mcqs.q: Which is the original trust territory under the Trusteeship System?
mcqs.explanation: Micronesia (Pacific Islands) was an original strategic trust territory administered by the USA.

mcqs.q: Which UN body shall exercise all functions relating to strategic areas?
mcqs.explanation: The Security Council exercises trusteeship functions for strategic areas.

mcqs.q: In which country's independence did the UN General Assembly take initiative from South Africa?
mcqs.explanation: The UN took over administration of Namibia (then South-West Africa) from South Africa, leading to independence in 1990.

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of the International Finance Corporation (IFC)?
mcqs.explanation: IFC, part of the World Bank Group, is headquartered in Washington D.C.

mcqs.q: When did Namibia become an independent country after UN efforts?
mcqs.explanation: Namibia gained independence on 21 March 1990.

mcqs.q: When was the first restructured substantive session of the Economic and Social Council held?
mcqs.explanation: The first restructured substantive ECOSOC session ran 27 April - 30 July 1992.

mcqs.q: In the UN, how many territories were placed under the Trusteeship System?
mcqs.explanation: Eleven trust territories were placed under the system, all of which have since become independent.

mcqs.q: Which Article of the UN Charter established the International Trusteeship System?
mcqs.explanation: Article 75 establishes the International Trusteeship System.

mcqs.q: Which is the first specialized agency of the UN?
mcqs.explanation: The International Labour Organization (ILO), founded 1919, was the first specialized agency of the UN.

mcqs.q: What are the main areas of work by the UN Population Fund (UNFPA)?
mcqs.explanation: UNFPA works on reproductive health, gender equality and environmental factors affecting population.

mcqs.q: Who are the members of the Trusteeship Council?
mcqs.explanation: The Trusteeship Council comprises the 5 permanent SC members (USA, UK, France, China, Russia).

mcqs.q: When were UN Peacekeeping Forces awarded the Nobel Peace Prize?
mcqs.explanation: UN Peacekeeping Forces won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1988.

mcqs.q: When was the International Labour Organization (ILO) established?
mcqs.explanation: The ILO was founded in 1919 under the Treaty of Versailles.

mcqs.q: What is the term of the Secretary-General of the UN?
mcqs.explanation: The Secretary-General serves a 5-year renewable term.

mcqs.q: Where is the head office of the International Labour Organization?
mcqs.explanation: ILO HQ is in Geneva, Switzerland.

mcqs.q: When was the ILO brought into relationship with the UN through a special agreement?
mcqs.explanation: In 1946 ILO became the first specialized agency of the UN.

mcqs.q: Which body supervised the entire election process leading to Namibia's independence?
mcqs.explanation: The UN supervised Namibia's pre-independence elections through UNTAG.

mcqs.q: The World Health Report is issued annually by:
mcqs.explanation: The annual World Health Report is published by the WHO.

mcqs.q: The World Development Report is issued annually by:
mcqs.explanation: The annual World Development Report is issued by the World Bank.

mcqs.q: The Division for Public Administration and Development Management is related with:
mcqs.explanation: DPADM is part of UN DESA (Department of Economic and Social Affairs).

mcqs.q: In which year was the International Civil Service Advisory Board established for the UN?
mcqs.explanation: The International Civil Service Advisory Board was established by the General Assembly in 1948.

mcqs.q: When did the General Assembly adopt the statute of the International Civil Service Commission?
mcqs.explanation: The ICSC Statute was adopted in December 1974.

mcqs.q: How many countries of industrial importance hold permanent seats on the ILO governing body?
mcqs.explanation: 10 'states of chief industrial importance' hold permanent seats on the ILO Governing Body.

mcqs.q: Which countries hold permanent seats on the governing body of the ILO?
mcqs.explanation: All listed countries are permanent ILO Governing Body members.

mcqs.q: Which UN Secretary-General was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize?
mcqs.explanation: Kofi Annan and the UN jointly won the 2001 Nobel Peace Prize. Dag Hammarskjöld also won posthumously in 1961.

mcqs.q: How many member countries are elected after every three years in the ILO?
mcqs.explanation: 17 member countries are elected to the ILO Governing Body every three years.

mcqs.q: In which year did the UN establish the International Maritime Organization (IMO)?
mcqs.explanation: The IMO began operating in 1959 (convention adopted 1948, in force 1958).

mcqs.q: Which of the following reports is published every year by the World Bank?
mcqs.explanation: The World Bank publishes the annual World Development Report.

mcqs.q: What are the main functions of the International Maritime Organization (IMO)?
options: Co-operation among governments on merchant shipping
Prevent marine pollution, facilitate maritime traffic
mcqs.explanation: The IMO covers shipping safety, maritime security and prevention of marine pollution.

mcqs.q: The Second World Conference on Human Rights was held in:
mcqs.explanation: The Second World Conference on Human Rights was held in Vienna in 1993.

mcqs.q: Which countries have the largest quotas of capital resources in the IMF?
mcqs.explanation: USA holds the largest IMF quota; Germany has historically been one of the largest contributors.

mcqs.q: How many members are in the International Law Commission?
mcqs.explanation: The International Law Commission has 34 members. (Note: original source gives 54.)

mcqs.q: How many executive directors are on the Board of Governors of the IMF?
mcqs.explanation: The IMF Executive Board has 24 executive directors.

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of the International Monetary Fund?
mcqs.explanation: IMF HQ is in Washington D.C., USA.

mcqs.q: Which nation has eligibility (by convention) to nominate the President of the World Bank?
mcqs.explanation: By convention, the World Bank President is traditionally a US national.

mcqs.q: The members of the International Law Commission are elected by:
mcqs.explanation: Members of the ILC are elected by the General Assembly.

mcqs.q: What are the aims of the International Monetary Fund?
options: Expansion of international trade and exchange rate stability
Removal of exchange restrictions, establishment of multilateral payment system
mcqs.explanation: All listed objectives are core IMF purposes.

mcqs.q: When was the International Telecommunications Union (ITU) established?
mcqs.explanation: ITU was established in 1865 — making it one of the oldest international organizations.

mcqs.q: When did the ITU become a Specialized Agency of the UN?
mcqs.explanation: The ITU became a UN specialized agency in 1947.

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of the International Telecommunications Union?
mcqs.explanation: ITU HQ is in Geneva, Switzerland.

mcqs.q: When did the Russian Federation take over the USSR's seat as permanent member of the Security Council?
mcqs.explanation: Russia inherited the USSR's permanent SC seat in December 1991 after the Soviet Union's dissolution.

mcqs.q: When was the UNESCO constitution signed in London?
mcqs.explanation: The UNESCO Constitution was signed in London on 16 November 1945.

mcqs.q: How many countries were in UNESCO when its constitution was signed on 16 November 1945?
mcqs.explanation: 37 countries signed the UNESCO constitution at its founding.

mcqs.q: Established by the GA in 1966, UNIDO became a UN Specialized Agency in:
mcqs.explanation: UNIDO became a specialized UN agency in 1985.

mcqs.q: How do UNESCO's primary objectives contribute to peace and security?
options: Rule of law, human rights and fundamental freedoms
mcqs.explanation: UNESCO promotes peace through education, science, culture, communication and human rights.

mcqs.q: On which subjects can individuals be accused by the International Criminal Court?
mcqs.explanation: The ICC prosecutes genocide, crimes against humanity, war crimes and aggression.

mcqs.q: How many members are in the Executive Board which governs the UN Development Programme (UNDP)?
mcqs.explanation: The UNDP Executive Board has 36 members.

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of UNESCO?
mcqs.explanation: UNESCO is headquartered in Paris, France.

mcqs.q: In which countries has the UNESCO Science Cooperation Office been set up?
mcqs.explanation: UNESCO has science cooperation offices in Cairo, Jakarta, Nairobi, New Delhi, Montevideo and Venice.

mcqs.q: How many member countries are there on the Executive Board of UNESCO?
mcqs.explanation: The UNESCO Executive Board has 58 member countries.

mcqs.q: What are UNESCO's focus areas in the cultural field?
options: Conserve and protect world cultural inheritance
Assisting member states in preserving cultural heritage
mcqs.explanation: UNESCO's cultural mission covers all of these — World Heritage, intangible heritage and cultural development.

mcqs.q: Whose part is the International Bureau of Education?
mcqs.explanation: The International Bureau of Education (IBE) is part of UNESCO, based in Geneva.

mcqs.q: When did the UN General Assembly establish the UN Industrial Development Organization (UNIDO)?
mcqs.explanation: UNIDO was established in 1966.

mcqs.q: What are the aims of the UN Industrial Development Organization (UNIDO)?
options: Helps developing countries fight marginalization and poverty
Mobilizes knowledge and technology for competitive economy
mcqs.explanation: UNIDO promotes inclusive and sustainable industrial development in developing countries.

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of UNIDO?
mcqs.explanation: UNIDO is headquartered in Vienna, Austria.

mcqs.q: When was the Universal Postal Union (UPU) established?
mcqs.explanation: UPU was established in 1874.

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of the Universal Postal Union?
mcqs.explanation: UPU HQ is in Bern, Switzerland.

mcqs.q: When did the World Health Organization (WHO) come into force?
mcqs.explanation: WHO came into force on 7 April 1948 — celebrated as World Health Day.

mcqs.q: What are the functions of the World Health Organization (WHO)?
options: Assist governments to strengthen health services
mcqs.explanation: WHO covers all these areas plus global health emergency response.

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of the World Health Organization?
mcqs.explanation: WHO HQ is in Geneva, Switzerland.

mcqs.q: Which of the following was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize three times?
options: International Committee of the Red Cross
mcqs.explanation: The International Committee of the Red Cross has won the Nobel Peace Prize three times (1917, 1944, 1963).

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of the Pan American Health Organization (PAHO)?
mcqs.explanation: PAHO HQ is in Washington D.C., USA. It serves as WHO's Regional Office for the Americas.

mcqs.q: In which year did WIPO become a UN Specialized Agency?
mcqs.explanation: WIPO became a UN specialized agency in 1974.

mcqs.q: How many organizations are under the UN system?
mcqs.explanation: The UN system comprises about 28 organizations (specialized agencies, programmes, funds and related).

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of the World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)?
mcqs.explanation: WIPO HQ is in Geneva, Switzerland.

mcqs.q: What is the official language of the World Intellectual Property Organization?
mcqs.explanation: WIPO operates in all 6 official UN languages.

mcqs.q: In which year was the World Meteorological Organization recognized as a specialized agency of the UN?
mcqs.explanation: WMO became a UN specialized agency in 1951 (the original answer key gives 1956 — alternative cited).

mcqs.q: When was the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) statute approved by the UN?
mcqs.explanation: The IAEA Statute was approved by the UN on 26 October 1956.

mcqs.q: Which is the UN agency associated with children's work?
mcqs.explanation: UNICEF — United Nations Children's Fund — is the UN agency for children's welfare.

mcqs.q: Where is the headquarters of the International Atomic Energy Agency?
mcqs.explanation: IAEA HQ is in Vienna, Austria.

mcqs.q: When did the World Trade Organization (WTO) come into force?
mcqs.explanation: The WTO came into force on 1 January 1995, replacing GATT.

mcqs.q: When did the UN General Assembly adopt the Universal Declaration of Human Rights?
mcqs.explanation: The UDHR was adopted on 10 December 1948 in Paris — celebrated as Human Rights Day.

mcqs.q: What does the Comprehensive Nuclear Test Ban Treaty (CTBT) prohibit?
options: Any other nuclear explosion anywhere in the world
mcqs.explanation: The CTBT prohibits both nuclear weapon test explosions and any other nuclear explosions worldwide.
